title: Fixing the Incorrect Prime Number Calculation

statement.markdown:
Given a range [l, r] and a number n, find all prime numbers in the range. Write a function to return a list of all such prime numbers. The function should return an empty list if there are no prime numbers in the range.

**Note:** You can assume that the input range is always within the valid range of integers and will not contain negative numbers.
statement.inputFormat: 2 integers l and r (inclusive) and an integer n
statement.outputFormat: A list of integers of prime numbers in the range [l, r]
statement.notes: The function should return all prime numbers in the range. It should not return duplicate primes.

examples.explanation: The prime numbers in the range [4, 10] are 5, 7.

hints.text: First, consider the case when n is 1. In this case, there are no prime numbers in the range [l, r].

editorial.title: Editorial Title
editorial.intuition: The function should iterate over the range [l, r] and check for each number if it is a prime number. To check for primality, we can use a helper function that checks if a number has any divisors other than 1 and itself.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach to find prime numbers in the range. This would involve checking each number in the range for primality.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm to find all prime numbers up to the square root of r.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution would be to use a more efficient algorithm such as the Sieve of Eratosthenes to find all prime numbers up to the square root of r.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for the optimal solution is that it correctly identifies all prime numbers in the range [l, r].
editorial.dryRun:
Dry running the optimal solution with the input 1
4 10 3
3 5 7 11
 should give us the correct output of 3 5 7 11
.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle the case when n is 1
Using an inefficient algorithm such as brute force

functionSignature.cpp: vector<int> solve(int n, pair<int, int> range, vector<int> lst);
functionSignature.java: List<Integer> solve(int n, Pair<Integer, Integer> range, List<Integer> lst);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, n, range, lst: list[int]) -> list[int]:
